---
title: "1 | Welcome to MaCSS!"
format: html
editor: visual
---

::: callout-tip
## Objectives This Week

- Introductions to the course, the professor, and the other students.
- Learn more about the goals of science, and why statistics is required for social scientists to help achieve these goals.
- Get started working with R and thinking about how you might want to use your knowledge of statistics as a social scientist.
:::

## Professor Says Hi

In this video, I say hi and introduce myself, provide an overview of the course (and the course materials we will be using to learn this summer), and a few highlights from the syllabus.

{{< video linkgoeshere >}}

## Assignments To-Do

1.  **Read the Introduction of Why Statistics.** In this chapter, you'll go over the goals of the course, and how to navigate the R programming language that we will be using to conduct statistics in this class.
2.  **Read Chapter 1 of Why Statistics.** In this chapter, you'll learn how and why science is used by people in positions of power to study variation in ways that help them gain knowledge and influence the world, and how to use the programming language R to define and graph a variable to illustrate variation.
3.  **Complete Quiz 1 (on bCourses).** This quiz reviews some of the key terminology from the Introduction and Chapter 1 of *Why Statistics* related to the science, statistics, and the programming language R. You'll also use R to answer a few questions. The quiz is open-note and allows for multiple attempts.
4.  [**Complete Lab X**]()**.** This week's lab assignment asks you to define and graph two variables in R, share your code, and then join our class discord (see bCourses for the link to join).
5.  **Check-Ins.** Each week, I'd also like you to complete a short surveys and reflections about the course. These check-ins will help me get a sense of how the course is going, help you connect course themes and concepts to real-life questions, and help us all connect with each other in this virtual and asynchronous classroom.
    1.  **Community Discussion Post on the [Vision Board](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1ekZrJ7PubP1h9zkEKDiOF1Q7eVWwd70ksuk2Wrd8pmk/edit?usp=sharing).** Post your responses to the community discussion prompts below on this week's vision board tab. Make sure to reply to a different student!
        - **YOUR POST :** What’s a question you have about the world? What variable is the focus of this question? What is the AFFECT, BEHAVIOR, and COGNITIVE aspect of this variable? What would between-person and within-person variation look like for this variable?

        - **YOUR REPLY :** Say Hi! Consider how knowledge of this topic might be used to PREDICT and / or CONTROL people’s outcomes. In other words, how could we use knowledge about this variable to impact the world?
    2.  **Take This Exit Survey.** Finally, a few questions to let me know how this first week of material went for you!

## Common Questions and Additional Resources

**I'm having trouble getting R to work!**

1.  Make sure you are downloading both R and RStudio. These are two separate programs you will need to install.
2.  Alternatively, you can set up a free account with posit.cloud. This is a cloud-based version of RStudio (and R). If you run out of free hours, you may need
3.  Reach out to me or the reader on discord, office hours

**I'm feeling overwhelmed (or bored!)**

1.  Resource.
2.  Resource.
